preparation_method: mixed with sweetened coconut milk sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard dessert serving of mango sticky rice with coconut milk is approximately 300 g, comprising steamed glutinous rice, fresh mango slices, and sweetened coconut milk sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Glutinous Rice (cooked), Mango (raw), Coconut Milk (canned), Sugar; composite dish estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for mango sticky rice with coconut milk as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked glutinous rice (FDC ID 168878), raw mango (FDC ID 169910), canned coconut milk (FDC ID 170172), and granulated sugar (FDC ID 169655), reflecting a typical Thai recipe ratio of approximately 50% glutinous rice, 25% mango, 20% coconut milk, and 5% sugar by weight. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with cross-check against the USDA component data. Singapore HPB database does not carry an entry for this Thai dish. FAO/INFOODS SEA table cited in the input record supports only menu listing and does not provide per-100g nutrient data for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for mango sticky rice with coconut milk as a composite dish. GI of 86 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous (waxy) rice, cooked (GI ~86, glucose reference), which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor in this dish. Mango contributes a lower GI (~51) and coconut milk has negligible carbohydrate GI impact; however, the glutinous rice component dominates the overall glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (86 × 75.0) / 100 = 64.5, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 25.0 × 3 = 75.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar (15 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1 g/100 g) and moderate saturated fat from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low at 50 mg/100 g, the high added sugar content (15 g/100 g) in this dessert promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, independently elevating blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced sugar in the coconut milk sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycaemic index (~86) from glutinous rice combined with 15 g/100 g added sugar and low dietary fibre (1 g/100 g), producing a glycaemic load of ~64.5 per standard 300 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mango sticky rice with coconut milk is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes; glutinous rice has one of the highest GI values of all rice varieties (~86), and combined with added sugar and a glycaemic load of ~64.5 per serving, it causes rapid and substantial postprandial blood glucose elevation that is difficult to manage with standard insulin or oral hypoglycaemic regimens.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.7 g/100 g from coconut milk (contributing ~20.1 g per 300 g serving) and high added sugar 15 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content from coconut milk (6.7 g/100 g, predominantly lauric and myristic acids) combined with high added sugar (15 g/100 g) raises LDL cholesterol and promotes endothelial inflammation; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dessert and consider fresh mango alone as a lower-risk alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.7 g/100 g from coconut milk exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; added sugar 15 g/100 g further promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dessert; the saturated fat from coconut milk (6.7 g/100 g) triggers TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and the high added sugar load independently amplifies systemic inflammation, outweighing the modest anti-inflammatory benefit of vitamin C from mango (10 mg/100 g).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango sticky rice with coconut milk (Thai: ข้าวเหนียวมะม่วง, romanized: Khao Niao Mamuang) is a classic Thai dessert consisting of steamed glutinous (waxy) rice that has been mixed with a sweetened, salted coconut milk sauce, served alongside slices of fresh ripe mango, and typically garnished with toasted sesame seeds. The glutinous rice is soaked overnight and steamed until tender and slightly chewy; it is then folded with warm coconut milk sweetened with palm or white sugar and a pinch of salt, which gives the rice a rich, creamy, mildly sweet flavour with a characteristic sticky, cohesive texture. The fresh mango provides a contrasting juicy, fibrous, and intensely sweet-tart flavour profile. Nutritionally, this dessert is energy-dense at approximately 183 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 25 g/100 g dominated by the very high glycaemic index glutinous rice (GI ~86), 15 g/100 g of added sugar, and 8.3 g/100 g of fat predominantly as saturated fat (6.7 g/100 g) from coconut milk. Dietary fibre is low at 1 g/100 g. A standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 549 kcal and a glycaemic load of ~64.5, making it clinically unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. It is widely available across Thailand, Southeast Asia, and in Thai restaurants globally, and is particularly popular during mango season (April to June in Thailand).

Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA FoodData Central component blend (glutinous rice, mango, coconut milk, sugar) and are broadly consistent with the input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for glutinous rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries (cooked glutinous rice FDC 168878, raw mango FDC 169910, canned coconut milk FDC 170172, granulated sugar FDC 169655) weighted at approximately 50:25:20:5 by mass. Input record values (ai_generated, grabfood provenance, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent and have been retained with minor rounding. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Thai dessert dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB database, or FAO/INFOODS SEA table as of 2024. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per USDA component data for these ingredients; input value of 0.03 g/100 g rounded to 0.0 as below meaningful detection threshold for this dish type). 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA coconut milk fatty acid profile; these are approximate. 5. The FAO/INFOODS SEA source cited in the input record supports only menu listing and does not provide per-100g nutrient data for this dish; it has not been used as a nutrient evidence source. 6. Ethnic tags 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai' in the input record are incorrect for this dish and have been removed. 7. country_of_origin in the input record was listed as Singapore; corrected to Thailand as this is a Thai-origin dish. 8. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct composite dish GI entry exists. Glycaemic load per 300 g serving = (86 × 75.0) / 100 = 64.5 g.
